coredata.dc:title: Electrically conductive coals in nature: Their traits, whereabouts, green-exploitations and impacts
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Busheng
Chen, Hongming
Han, Zhiqiang
Song, Liyin
Liu, Zijin
Lau, Woon-Ming
Zhou, Dan
coredata.dc:description: Amid the global advocation of carbon-neutrality, high-carbon-footprint-combustion is still catastrophically depleting the natural coal-reserves. Parallelly, high-value graphitic nanomaterials, from low-end graphitic-nano-grains known as carbon black to high-end polymorphs of carbon nanofiber, carbon nanotube and graphene, have been invented to fuel an emerging economy with innovations emphasizing profitability instead of sustainability. Here, by analyzing the coal-traits and the relevant geological metamorphisms, we adopt the portrait of coal as a carbonaceous composite comprising graphitic nano-grains, typically merely 1–2 nm in size and not more than 20 % in concentration, in a matrix of amorphous carbonaceous macromolecules high in aromaticity, dusted by mineral-specks, with weak intermolecular links among all constituents. At the proximity of geological faults, metamorphism, carbonization and graphitization were once accelerated to form more graphitic nano-grains with crystallinity and electrical conductivity enhanced. Thus, coal-seams near such fault-dikes likely have electrically conductive coals full of percolated graphitic nano-grains with weak grain-boundary-links such that coal can be comminuted to graphitic nanomaterials without any chemical etch, with little loss, low cost and minimal environmental-load. Empowered by these views, we have found over twenty coal-fields in China and many more elsewhere having such conductive coals, with trials from lattice-imaging characterization to ton-scale production to validate our predictions. We trust that sharing such concepts and experimental findings promises new vehicles embracing sustainability to vitalize the coal-industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrically conductive coals
High-value graphitic nanomaterials
Minimal environmental-load

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foshan Science and Technology Innovation Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support from Scientific and Technological Innovation Foundation of Shunde Innovation School, USTB ( BK21BE010 ), Foshan Science and Technology Innovation Project (no. 2018IT100363 ) and Foshan Talents Special Foundation ( BKBS202003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural Analysis of Conductive Coal. (a) Computational simulations of anthracite having 20 wt% of graphitic nano-grains; (b) HRTEM of Coal #1; (c) Computational simulations of the microstructures revealed by the HRTEM of Coal#1; (d) Raman spectrum of Coal #1.

Preparation schematic diagram of conductive coal based nano carbon materials. First, use a roller crusher to crush the coal to 0.5–1 cm, and then use a pulverizer to crush the electric coal to below 100 μm. When preparing nano-particles, firstly use zirconia beads with diameter of 6 mm to wet crush to 1 μm, then use zirconia beads with diameter of 0.6 mm to wet crush to 0.2 μm, and finally use zirconia beads with diameter of 0.1 mm to reach the ideal nanometer size.

Schematic diagram of the formation mechanism of conductive coal. (a) Fault extrusion as a thermal heat-source for accelerating of metamorphorism. (b) Historical tectonic heat supply and the consequential changes in the mean-random reflectance.

Distribution map of conductive coal. (a) Global locations of conductive coals (The star represents the confirmed existence, and the black dot represents the probable existence.). (b) Predicted map of locations for getting conductive coals in the Donets Basin. (c) Confirmed locations for getting conductive coals from the suburb of the Handan City in the Hebei Province in China.
Analysis of residual coal on a gangue rock from the Tashan coal mine in the Shanxi Province in China. (a) Raman spectrum, (b) HRTEM.
HRTEM of a rectangular stack of vertically aligned graphene layers found from the residual coal on a gangue rock from the Tashan coal-mine.
The Vitrinite reflectance, electrical conductivity and XRD analysis results of each coal sample were obtained.

head.ce:title: Electrically conductive coals in nature: Their traits, whereabouts, green-exploitations and impacts

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, conductive coal worldwide was discovered and identified to prepare high-value nano carbon materials using environmentally friendly route, which offers a green and sustainable solution for the novel utilization of coal and carbon-neutrality.

Amid the global advocation of carbon-neutrality, high-carbon-footprint-combustion is still catastrophically depleting the natural coal-reserves. Parallelly, high-value graphitic nanomaterials, from low-end graphitic-nano-grains known as carbon black to high-end polymorphs of carbon nanofiber, carbon nanotube and graphene, have been invented to fuel an emerging economy with innovations emphasizing profitability instead of sustainability. Here, by analyzing the coal-traits and the relevant geological metamorphisms, we adopt the portrait of coal as a carbonaceous composite comprising graphitic nano-grains, typically merely 1–2 nm in size and not more than 20 % in concentration, in a matrix of amorphous carbonaceous macromolecules high in aromaticity, dusted by mineral-specks, with weak intermolecular links among all constituents. At the proximity of geological faults, metamorphism, carbonization and graphitization were once accelerated to form more graphitic nano-grains with crystallinity and electrical conductivity enhanced. Thus, coal-seams near such fault-dikes likely have electrically conductive coals full of percolated graphitic nano-grains with weak grain-boundary-links such that coal can be comminuted to graphitic nanomaterials without any chemical etch, with little loss, low cost and minimal environmental-load. Empowered by these views, we have found over twenty coal-fields in China and many more elsewhere having such conductive coals, with trials from lattice-imaging characterization to ton-scale production to validate our predictions. We trust that sharing such concepts and experimental findings promises new vehicles embracing sustainability to vitalize the coal-industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:sections.ce:para: Nature-based solution to pave the phasedown of coal-combustion. Within four decades the mandates of carbon-neutrality are expected to be internationally complied [1], with the emergence of many interruptive changes in industry and society. Particularly, clean energy will replace the current fossil-based energy supplies having high carbon footprints, a replacement which will impact the global consumption of primary energy currently amounted to about $7 trillion dollars. In fact, the recent relevant global policy-decisions[1,2], most notably the Glasgow Climate Pack[2]collectively agreed by 197 countries in late 2021, specifically calls for world-wide government-led efforts towards the phasedown of unabated coal power[3]. Further, to fix current climate-change issues but not at the expense of other measures bearing negative ecological impacts and liabilities, the concept of nature-based solutions[4–6] has emerged and been advocated for enhancing the practicality and effectiveness of these policy-driven measures. The present work stresses that the designs and implementations of measures managing the phasedown and phaseout of coal-combustion must also align with the concept of nature-based solutions for longevity. Further, we view that the orderly replacement of coal power by clean electricity is practical in both technology and economy. However, there are still no adequate green technologies and technology-transfers to address the relevant losses induced by the phasedown of coal-combustion.

Specifically, some 8 billion tons of coal is consumed annually around the world, with a 70 % portion used to generate electricity and heat, and the rest for other low-value-added applications such as coking coal for the industry of metal refinery and pyrolyzing coals for the industry of chemicals production. Thus, phasing down coal power and coal combustion with no strategic plan for raising high-value-added green-exploitations of natural coal will negatively impact most stakeholders of the current trillion-dollar coal-economy derived from coal combustion. As such, high resistance against the policy-driven changes is inevitable and some irrational responses may even cause a rise rather than a drop in carbon footprints. In this context, a nature-based solution to address this very critical issue must holistically satisfy the following practical considerations:
•
Abandoning the conventional view that coal is a cheap source of carbon atoms, together with oxygen and hydrogen, for low-value-added combustion and heat generation. Instead, respecting coal as a family of carbonaceous products well engineered by nature and, therefore, exploiting the highest-value-added applications of such traits.
•
Complying with the principles of green-engineering[7,8]in coal exploitation, with special attentions to restraining the use of chemicals and energy, raising the production yield, and minimizing the environmental load.
•
Assessing the economic impact of the new coal knowledge and applications, and thus their practical opportunities in balancing the hundred-billion losses in coal-economy incurred by phasing down coal combustion.

Proposed solution: finding conductive coal and milling it to high-value nanocarbons. Recent advances in science and technology have indeed provided good evidence that such a nature-based solution is both technically and economically plausible. First, high-value graphitic nano-grains in the forms of fullerene[9], carbon nano-onion[10], carbon nanotube[11,12], carbon nanofiber[13,14], and graphene[15]have been invented, all with a current price over 100 times that of coal. Second, decades of coal studies[16–21]have revealed that coal is derived from versatile metamorphisms of buried algae and plant remains driven by geological heat and pressure to form, with an increasing degree of carbonization and graphitization, peat < lignite < bituminous coal < anthracite. Projecting such a rich set of knowledge[16–22]to the preceding perspective, we portrait the geological metamorphisms, chemical reactions which first transform organic macromolecules to polycyclic aromatic compounds (PACs) and then to graphitic nano-grains, as effective engineering processes highly optimized by nature not for supplying combustible materials but for storing carbonaceous materials having high stability. Such natural resources are thus best exploited for the emerging value-chains relying on a good supply of graphitic nano-grains[9–15]. In this portrait, common coal is a carbonaceous composite comprising graphitic nano-grains, typically merely 1–2 nm in size and not more than 20 % in concentration, in a matrix of amorphous carbonaceous macromolecules rich in PACs, dusted by mineral-specks, with weak intermolecular links among all constituents. Such a simplified model of common coal is depicted in Fig. 1 a. Furthermore, we emphasize that at the proximity of geological faults, metamorphism, carbonization and graphitization were once accelerated to form more graphitic nano-grains with crystallinity and electrical conductivity enhanced. Thus, coal-seams near such fault-dikes are locations where electrically conductive (therein “electrically/electrical” is omitted for simplicity) coals full of percolated graphitic nano-grains can be found. Since these nano-grains are bounded merely with weak grain-boundary-links, such conductive coal can be comminuted to graphitic nano-grains without any chemical etch, with little loss, and is low cost and carries minimal environmental-load.

Traits and identification of conductive coals. Coals are geochemically derived from plant remains with various degrees of carbonization and graphitization [16–19]; hence, international standards have been erected to classify coals. For example, under the ISO Standard 11,760 - Classification of Coals[23], coals are categorized, from low to high rank to mark their degrees of metamorphism, in accord to the mean-random-reflectance (Rm) benchmark: lignite @Rm < 0.4 %; subbituminous coal @Rm ≥ 0.4 % to < 0.5 %; bituminous coal @Rm ≥ 0.5 % to < 2.0 %; anthracite @Rm ≥ 2.0 % to < 6.0 %. The respective carbon contents in bituminous/subbituminous coal and anthracite, excluding moisture in coal, are typically 45–80 % and 80–90 %. Thorough X-ray diffraction, lattice-imaging transmission electron microscopy and structural modelling [16–22,24–31] have consistently revealed that all common coal types, except meta-anthracite which is a high-rank sub-category of anthracite with Rm ≥ 4.0 %, are amorphous with typically < 20 % graphitic constituents which are known to have a typical basal plane dimension (La) of 1–2 nm and stack-thickness (Lc) of 1 nm. As such, natural coal is typically insulating and little attention has been paid to the conductivity of coal. However, with our proposed strategic drive towards high-yield production of high-value graphite nano-grains from nature coal, we advocate screening coal with conductivity as a benchmark simply because conductive coal must comprise mainly of conductive graphitic nano-grains with a concentration high enough to reach percolation. Distilling from a large database of our experimental results in reference to both characterization and industrial applications of coals relevant to this strategic drive, we further propose to define conductive coal as a natural coal with a virgin conductivity of not less than 1 μS/cm.

Some exemplary data are highlighted in Table 1 , to set a stage for further disseminations of the traits and impacts of conductive coal. Clearly, conductivity is highly correlated to other benchmarking coal-attributes such as Rm, La, and crystalline/amorphous ratio. Further, meta-anthracite, which has the highest degree of graphitization among all coal, is the best candidate for high-yield production of high-value graphitic nano-grains. In reference to the practicality of setting conductivity as a new benchmark, since testing conductivity requires only a simple multimeter, identifying conductive coal for a practical and profitable production of graphitic nano-grains is easy and inexpensive.

Multi-billion market of green graphitic nano-grains from conductive coal. The market has indeed been craving for such new green-production of graphitic nano-grains at low cost, as nanocarbon polymorphs [9–15]are currently produced with rather high production costs and environmental loads. At the low-price-end with a high market-volume, some 15 million tons of graphitic nano-grains with a typical primary particular-size of 10–50 nm, the atomic fine-structure of which comprises an agglomerate of 1–3 nm nanocrystals each having a few atomic-layers of nano-graphene sheets, are annually produced and sold with a trade-name of carbon-black[32–34]. Most carbon-black materials are exploited as fillers and reinforcers in the rubber composites of automobile-tyres[34], with a market-price of about $1500/ton to build a market of about $20 billion. Traditionally, carbon-black is produced by atomizing and incompletely-combusting coal-tar which is a by-product when coal is pyrolyzed into coke. Alternatively, acetylene, also derived from natural coal and oil, is thermally decomposed to carbon black. Although the environmental load of producing acetylene-derived carbon-black is considerably low than that of the coal-tar-derived one, it is by no mean conformal to the principles of green chemistry. As such, when conductive coal is available, simple and comminuting it to carbon black is a good exemplar of a nature-based solution for establish a multi-billion market of high-value-added exploitations of coal. Further, high purity and electrically-conductive carbon-blacks are currently exploited, with a relatively high profitability, in modifying/producing batteries [35].

The second best in reference to volume-consumption of nano-grains is the production of carbon nanotube[11,12], also mainly for the emerging battery industry[35]. Carbon nanotubes are typically catalytically grown by the catalytic dissociation of methane or acetylene. The cost of such a growth can be suppressed to some $5000/ton but the purification cost and environmental load are typically much higher. The final example of synthetic nano-grain is graphene[15]which is, for a typical structural specification of a sheet-size in microns and a thickness of less than 3 nm (10 graphitic basal lattice-planes), typically priced at over $150,000/ton. Such graphene nano-grains are practically produced with the Hammers method[36] in which graphite is grinded to micro-powder and then chemical exfoliation via oxidative expanding the basal-plane-edges of micro-graphite. Such a production consumes a massive amount of corrosive chemicals for both the exfoliation and purification of graphene, consumption which costs some $50,000 for the production of a ton of graphene in reference to chemical supplies and industrial waste-managements. In short, graphitic nano-grains are artificially produced with high costs and high environmental loads, and innovations of their green-production at low cost are urgently demanded.

Examples of practical ton-scale-production of nanocarbons from natural coal have indeed been readily reported. For example, humic acids which are large organic molecules rich in PACs and organic-acid-functionality with a good applicability in the fertilizer-industry, are produced[37]by milling the low-rank coal of lignite. The production takes advantage of the natural metamorphism of plant remains into the formation of such humic acids[24,37]. In reference to linking natural coal to the production of high-value graphitic nano-grains, the innovative works by Tour and coworkers[38]are seminal, as they demonstrated that graphene-like fluorescent quantum-dots promising high-value optoelectronic applications can be leached from bituminous coal and anthracite by oxidative etching the non-graphitic constituents. Subsequently, other researchers[39]further showed that basically all coal types can be used to leach out PACs resembling nano-graphene with a turbostratic basal lattice-sheet, plus multiple stacks of such nano-graphene, again by oxidatively etching the non-graphitic constituents. Inspired by these innovations[38,39], as well as the rich coal literature[16–19,40–45]including maps of coal fields having geological faults[40–45], and projecting them into the wholistic concept of natural-based solutions, we have found over twenty coal-fields in China and many more elsewhere having conductive coal and have practically comminuted exemplary conductive coals into high-value graphitic nano-grains.

In summary, the preceding background analyses outline the current critical issues in reference to vitalize the coal-industry with a mission of promoting carbon neutrality, and introduce our research strategies and goals to address such issues by the proposition of green and high-yield production of high-value graphitic nano-grains from conductive coal. In the following sections, we disclose, analyze and articulate our results on exploring the traits and whereabouts of conductive coal, and on exploiting such conductive coal for the green production of graphitic nano-grains with an encouraging impact on the coal-economy.

ce:section.ce:para: Critical features of conductive coal. The core of our technology innovation is setting an unconventional attribute of electric conductivity to quickly gauge the presence of highly percolated conductive graphitic nano-grains in a natural coal specimen, and thereby the production-yield of graphitic nano-grains with such coal. To facilitate both our understanding of such conductive coal and to project the rich coal literature to the traits of conductive coals, this new benchmark of electrical conductivity (σ) must be compared with the common benchmarking coal-attributes such as reflectance (Rm), the graphitic crystallinity (L a , L c , and crystalline/amorphous ratio). The comparative results, as summarized in Table 1, reveals a positive Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.69 between ln(σ) and Rm, a comparison which is further clarified in the Supplementary Section. This observed correlation hints that high-rank coals such as meta-anthracite are likely conductive and thus preferred for the high-yield production of graphitic nano-grains. Further, a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.85 is found between ln(σ) and L a , and that for ln(σ) and L c is 0.78. Indeed, conductivity along the basal planes is known to be about a thousand times higher than that along the c-axis of graphite. The observed correlations link quite convincingly the bulk conductivity of a conductive coal specimen to the microscopic conductivity in its graphitic nano-grains. Further, the percolation of such graphitic nano-grains, with an inter-grain separation close enough for electron tunneling is a prerequisite of the presence of a bulk conductivity. In comparison, low-rank coal types mainly comprising aggregates of turbostratic PACs with insulating intermolecular bridges are non-conductive. They have been shown to be suitable for the production of fluorescent nano-graphene quantum dots [38,39]but, in our opinion, are not suitable for the production of graphitic nano-grains in the context of the present work which aims at developing massive green-exploitations of coal in multiple industrial sectors.

The atomic-scaled microstructure of Coal #1, a meta-anthracite specimen showing the highest conductivity of 66.7 S/cm among all conductive coals in the dataset in Table 1, is revealed by the high-resolution transmission-electron-micrograph (HRTEM) in Fig. 1b. Clearly, nano-grains each comprising an assembly of randomly oriented crystalline domains, are the main constituents in this conductive coal. Further lattice analyses confirm that a crystalline domain of this microstructure typically has a stack of several turbostratic graphene layers with basal-plane sizes ranging from 1 to 15 nm. Since the total carbon content is 93 % in this particular sample and since no mineral specks are spotted in Fig. 1b, the inter-grain spaces in Fig. 1b are filled with amorphous carbonaceous moieties or voids. The micrograph also reveals that conductive Coal #1 indeed comprises highly percolated graphitic nano-grains with most inter-grain separations close enough for electron tunneling/hopping, a microstructure which leads to the observed bulk conductivity of 66.7 S/cm. Similar HRTEMs have been reported [25–31]in studies of meta-anthracite and pyrolyzed anthracite, and they all pictorially support that meta-anthracite and pyrolyzed anthracite comprise percolated assemblies of graphitic nano-grains. As such, these specimens are also conductive.

A computational simulation of the observed poly-grain microstructure in Fig. 1b is included in Fig. 1c. In this simulation, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen atoms are included to reflect the compositional details as experimentally measured from Coal #1. From this particular set of computational simulations, we count about 5000 carbon atoms constituting all the grain-boundaries in the 2-D plot of Coal #1. Among them, most are terminated by C–H or carbon-chains with no connections to other neighboring grains; as such, the corresponding inter-grain binding forces are van der Waals in nature. Some 500 of them are terminated with C–C, C-O-C or C-N-C inter-grain-links. This set of simulation data thus helps to justify our choice of freeing graphitic nano-grains from conductive coals simply by mechanical commination, and our rejection of any chemical means of oxidatively etching and consuming the amorphous carbon atoms in the proximity of all grain-boundaries.

In the literature, similar models[17–22] derived from the atomic composition of coal have also been drawn to describe the nano-crystallinity of anthracite, and these previous studies have confirmed the validity of the modeling approach in reference to the known crystallinity characterization of coal by XRD and HRTEM. The model in Fig. 1c, which is derived from the experimental observations displayed by the HRTEM of a conductive coal, is microscopically similar to the simulation model (Fig. 1a) of anthracite having merely 20 wt% of graphitic nano-grain and plausibly electrically nonconductive. The only difference between them is that the microstructure in Fig. 1a is a precursor of that in Fig. 1c, and they become the same when the nonconductive anthracite is subjected to further metamorphism.

Raman spectroscopy is widely used for studying the microstructure of coal[16–19], and thus a Raman spectrum of Coal #1 is included in Fig. 1d and Raman spectra for the other coal samples in Table 1 are included in Supplementary Fig. 1. Again, all Raman spectra support the presence of graphitic nano-grains in conductive coal.

Practical production of graphitic nano-grains with conductive coal. Indeed, all conductive coals in Table 1 have been successfully grinded to particulates as small as 35 nm in diameter with relatively low energy consumption and low loss. The typical production process is articulated in Fig. 2 , with a schematic diagram of an automatic production facility included in Supplementary Fig. 2 to demonstrate the practical pilot-production of 400 tons annually. In such a production process, conductive coals are typically crushed to less than 100 µm and wet-comminated first to 1 µm in size with zirconia beads of 6 mm in diameter, then to 0.2 µm in size with zirconia beads of 0.6 mm in diameter, and finally to the desirable nano-size with zirconia beads of 0.1 mm in diameter. The ultimate size-limit with this simple commination technology is governed by the surface roughness of the smallest zirconia beads available in the market at a practical cost. From our trials, we estimate that the total production cost for a graphitic nano-grain slurry with a grain-size matching that of the mid-size carbon black in the market is less than $700 per ton of dry graphitic nano-grains. The energy-consumption is less than 2000 kW·h per ton. However, drying a water-based slurry is high in energy consumption; for a slurry with 10 % of solid by weight, such energy consumption can be more than 7500 kW·h per ton of dry graphitic nano-grains. Although some of the heat can be recycled, this energy consumption is clearly an unacceptable attribute in our proposed green-technology. Currently we are developing an alternative commination approach with a non-polar and industrially safe organic solvent to replace water, with the aim of drastically reducing the energy consumption for the production of dry graphitic nano-grains. The replacement of water with a non-polar liquid also promises the relief of the massive and costly reliance of surfactants required to disperse the graphitic nano-grains in water. The replacement is also expected to raise the nano-grain content in the comminated slurry higher than the current technical limit of 10 %. These promising cost and environmental-load advantages have indeed already been preliminarily confirmed in our trials and are further optimized in reference particularly to the recycling of the non-polar solvent.

In comparison, the energy consumption for producing a ton of carbon black is typically about 2400 kg of coal-equivalent (kgce). Equating the equivalence of 1 kgce to 8.167 kW·h, we estimate an energy consumption of about 19,000 kW·h which is much higher than that for our proposed production via comminating conductive coal. Yet, our approach production of promises not only a reduction in energy consumption but also a disruptive advantage in reducing pollutant-emissions over the traditional production of carbon black by pyrolysis of coal to coal-tar and by atomizing and partial combustion of coal-tar to graphitic nano-grains.

A key feature highlighted by the dataset in Table 1 is the broad range of conductivity in natural coals from different coal-fields: from 0.02 μS/cm to 67 S/cm. Clearly, a post-commination method for tuning the conductivity to meet the specific market demand is required. By tracking the TGA dynamics and conductivity-rise as a function of sintering temperature, we find the threshold of thermal increase in conductivity at about 800 °C, as shown in Table 1. The associated TGA data, as shown in Supplementary Fig. 3, show that emission of H2O takes place at 100–200 °C, carbonization of Coal #1 and emission of H and O start at about 500 °C, and the onset of conductivity-rise at 700 °C signals graphitization at its earliest stage. The rise in conductivity and the statistical increases in L a and L c support this assertion. From our trials in matching the market demands of electrically conductive graphitic nano-grains for production with raw coals having relatively low conductivity (less than 1 μS/cm), the temperature range of post-commination thermal enhancement of conductivity can be as low as 800 °C as shown by the change in conductivity of Coal #7 in Table 1. Hence, this post-commination process is practical economically, particularly because customers with strict conductivity-specifications are willing to pay much more than the market price of ordinary carbon black having no specifications in conductivity. In the era of carbon neutrality, the post-commination pyrolysis process is powered by clean electricity, to satisfy the mission of curbing carbon footprints.

Locations of conductive coals in China and elsewhere. Once the correlations of conductivity to the common coal-attributes of reflectance and XRD graphitic crystallinity are established, as shown in Table 1, finding conductive coals globally is readily assisted by the literature data describing global coal-fields[40–45]with these two coal-attributes. To facilitate future research and exploitation of conductive coal and the production of coal-based graphitic nano-grains, we propose to set the conductivity of not less than 1 μS/cm and the mean-random-reflectance of not less than 3 % as qualifying criteria for classifying coals as electrically conductive coal. In compliance with the international standard for classifying coals, coals with Rm ≥ 3 % are commonly known as meta-anthracite the formation of which has been quite well-documented[16–19,25–31,40–45]. Briefly, the deep metamorphism to yield high-rank-coals of anthracite and meta-anthracite requires the historical natural occurrence of tectonic activities to warrant the supply of sufficiently high thermal energy, preferably with shear-force, for a duration typically measured with the unit of million-years[40–45]. A set of such background descriptions is pictorially shown in Fig. 3 . Hence, a coal-seam hit by a major geological fault can also be used, together with Rm ≥ 3 %, as a search-light for finding conductive coals globally.

With these searching tools, we have found in China more than twenty coal-fields from which we have indeed confirmed experimentally their good reserves of conductive coals. Among them, the Taixi coal-field in the Ningxi Region has, for many decades, supplied internationally the best anthracite which contains unusually little ash, sulfur and phosphorus. On the other hand, the Fengfeng coal-field is our favorite as its descriptions are readily available in the literature[40], information which led us to find our first batch of conductive coals. With the same search-methodology, we have located, as shown in Fig. 4 , over 50 coal-fields around the world as good candidates for finding conductive coals. Particularly, the high density and intensity of violent fault intrusions, likely once involving also liquid magma, in the Donets Basin are rather unusual[41], and we speculate the Donets Basin as the world-largest reserve of conductive coal readily for the production of high-value graphitic nano-grains. There, the peak temperatures at the fault-dikes are believed to unusually reach 900–1000 °C, for a duration of 0.1–0.2 million years. This natural pyrolysis of coal is a patient version of the common pyrolysis of anthracite to highly conductive coke in the coal industry, typically with a short pyrolysis at 1500–2000 °C in daily production. Known geological studies [42–45] of other coal-fields show that a peak temperature as low as 300 °C, with the presence of sufficiently high shear-pressure, is also sufficient to drive the natural formation of anthracite and meta-anthracite.

Noteworthily, Mastalerz and coworkers[44]reported an insightful set of quantitative Rm measurements taken from a coal-seam in the Illinois Basin of USA as a function of the sampling distance from a geological fault-dike. Briefly, the original, unaltered coal at a distance of 4 m from the dike has Rm of 0.66 %, which signifies bituminous coal, whereas respective Rm at the dike and at 1.5 m are 5.4 % and 3.4 %. Similar phenomena were also observed and reported elsewhere [45]. As such, meta-anthracite, and thus conductive coals, can likely be found even in bituminous coal-seams near fault-dikes. More information on these interesting cases is summarized in Supplementary Fig. 4. The evidence of deep carbonization leading to meta-anthracite formation at the fault-dike interface with a bituminous coal-seam has inspired us to expand the present work to the exploration if the notorious solid-wastes of coal-gangues contain conductive coal suitable for green-production of high-value graphitic nano-grains because a fault-seam interface is definitely decorated by coal-gangues.

Gangue is not solid waste. The Tashan coal-mine [45] is one of the most modernized and largest coal-mines in China. Massively produced in the Shanxi Province, Tashan coals are basically bituminous coal, and accompanying the coal production is the generation of some 30 million tons of gangues. From one of its gangue piles, with its photo included in Supplementary Fig. 5, a gangue was randomly picked and the micro-Raman spectra randomly taken from the untreated gangue surface, with one of them shown in Fig. 5 a, and the rest shown in Supplementary Fig. 1, confirm the presence of graphitic nano-grains. Interestingly, the HRTEM of the residual coal scratched off from the gangue reveals, as shown in Fig. 5b, a versatile mix of nanocarbon polymorphs including carbon nano-onion, carbon nanotube, carbon nanofiber, and arguably graphene-sheets. These nanocarbon polymorphic mixtures can be screened and extracted by washing gangue and flotation. We speculate that iron oxides on the gangue were reduced by the neighboring carbonaceous constituents to metallic iron which catalyzed the formation of the nanocarbon polymorphs shown in Fig. 5b. A thorough search, however, fails the detection of any iron on the HRTEM sample of Fig. 5b, likely because after the catalytic growth of the nanocarbon polymorphs, all catalysts had been leached and completely consumed in a long duration of over a hundred million years.

To further enrich the intriguing nanocarbon polymorph production by our mother-nature, another HRTEM taken from the residual coal on the Tashan gangue, as shown in Fig. 6 , reveals the peculiar presence of a rack of graphite basal planes, with some 60 stacks of basal-planes each has a conformal plane-size of about 10 nm, to form a rectangle of 20 nm by 10 nm. In graphene research, similar stacks of vertically aligned graphene known as “graphene nano-walls [46]” have indeed been synthesized and they display HRTEMs similar to that in Fig. 6. However, their preparation requires meticulous steps including the epitaxial growth of 3C-SiC (111) on Si (111), the subsequent growth of 2H-SiC wall-like scaffolds having vertical lattice-stacks of Si/C/Si/C on 3C-SiC (111), and finally etching silicon from 2H-SiC with atomic hydrogen to yield graphene nanowalls. In the Tashan gangue case, we speculate that the tectonic activity at the fault dike comprising the Tashan gangue had driven the reduction of a speck of silicon dioxide by neighboring coal to form a thin 10 nm layer of 2H-SiC. In the million-year long post-growth period, silicon atoms were slowly oxidized and the oxide gradually leached, to form graphene nanowalls in Fig. 6. In our own laboratory, we indeed managed to form a 10 nm silicon carbide layer on a quartz crystal which was coated with the Tashan coal and sintered in a tube furnace in an inert gas atmosphere at 1200 °C. The evidence of the formation of silicon carbon in this experiment is given in Supplementary Fig. 6. Unfortunately, although the formation of SiC near a fault dike of the Tashan coal-mine can be mimicked in a laboratory, the natural preferential removal of the silicon atoms in the SiC layer by leaching is extremely slow and cannot be mimicked with our limited resources and capabilities. The nature wins.

Green strategies for exploiting non-conductive coals. In closing, we further propose a green version of coking coal with the following set of design-rules so that stakeholders in the coal industry with no availability of meta-anthracite can still participate in the production of high-value graphitic nanocarbons:
•
The conventional coking temperature in the present industry is typically 950–1000 °C, and the temperature for pyrolyzing anthracite to conductive meta-anthracite is typically 1500–2000 °C. In comparison, Table 1 shows that coal #7, whose original conductivity does not meet the standard of conductive coal, still increases its conductivity by 5 times under heat treatment at 800℃, although it does not meet the set standard of conductive coal. Thus, green-engineering a low-rank coal to conductive coal should adopt a minimum pyrolysis-temperature which is lower than that for conventionally coking/pyrolyzing coal (Supplementary Fig. 7). Excessive heating not only consumes more energy but also compromises the grindability of the conductive coal. Similar optimized pyrolysis of natural biomaterials to high-value nanocarbons are also known to be practical [47].
•
Once the green production of graphitic nano-grains by comminuting the resultant conductive coal is accomplished, the resultant nano-grain product can be further treated, including a second step of minimal pyrolysis, to tailor the product conductivity and other properties for satisfying the specific market demands.

The degree of carbonization and graphitization of coal seams at the proximity of geological faults are found to be high enough for raising the contents of electrically conductive graphitic nano-grains into percolated aggregates with mechanically weak inter-grain links. Such conductive coal, readily produced by nature with a considerably high natural abundance and total reserve, can thus be easily comminuted for the high-yield production of high-value graphitic nanocarbons at low cost and low environmental load. The green exploitations include replacing the pollutive production of carbon black and addressing the emerging demands of conductive nanocarbons by the battery industry, with market volumes in multiple sectors high enough to offset the inevitable losses in the coal-economy due to policy-driven phasing down of coal-combustion.

The experimental anthracites were provided by Jizhong Energy Fengfeng Group Co., Ltd. (#1 and #3), Yangquan Coal Industry (Group) Co., Ltd. (#2 and #5), Ningxia Shizuishan Mining (Group) Co., Ltd (#6), Guizhou Liupanshui Water Mining Group (#4) and Yongan Coal Mine Yiliang County, Yunnan Province (#7). The ball milling medium comprised yttrium stabilized zirconia beads (CZY0010 particle size 0.08 ∼ 0.13 mm and 0.6 ∼ 0.8 mm) purchased from South Korea Sanuo Co., Ltd. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (industrial grade) was purchased from Guangdong Yuemei Chemical.

Roll crusher and pulverizer were used to crush large lump coal to the particle size below 100 um. Pulverized coal, polyvinyrrolidone (dispersant) and water (1:0.5:10) were then put into a container and mixed well with a mechanical agitator before grinding to less than 1 um with a mixing ball mill. Finally, zirconia beads with a diameter of 0.6 mm and 0.1 mm were used to ground the processed coal powder to below 40 nm in a rod-cutting agitator mill (Supplementary Fig. 2). The raw coal powder pulverized to more than 400 mesh was put into a tube furnace filled with argon or nitrogen and heated for 2 h at different temperatures, respectively. The electrical conductivity at a pressure of 4 Mpa was measured using a four-probe grinding resistance meter. The SiC layer was prepared by coating a quartz plate with a slurry of coal-based nanocarbon and then by heating the coated quartz in a vacuum tube furnace at 1200 ℃ for 2 h.

The specimen atomic microstructures were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (FEI Tecnai F20). The element types were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). The XRD patterns were acquired using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D2 PHASER). The particle size distributions were measured by a laser particle size analyzer (Bettersize 2600). The graphitic nature of the coal specimens were analyzed by Raman spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific DXR3xi). The mass-temperature curves of coal specimens were obtained by a thermogravimetric analyzer (PerkinElmer TGA 8000). The vitrinite reflectance data of coal specimens were determined in compliance with the standard for measuring vitrinite reflectance of coal (GB/T 6948–2008). The electrical conductivity of coal specimens were tested by a Four-probe powder conductivity measuring instrument (ROOKO FT-8100).

Busheng Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Hongming Chen: Methodology, Investigation. Zhiqiang Han: Software, Data curation. Liyin Song: Data curation. Zijin Liu: Data curation. Woon-Ming Lau: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Dan Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from Scientific and Technological Innovation Foundation of Shunde Innovation School, USTB (BK21BE010), Foshan Science and Technology Innovation Project (no.2018IT100363) and Foshan Talents Special Foundation (BKBS202003).
